quality.rationale: 长物理链条：牛顿轨道力学结合四极矩公式推导引力辐射导致的双星轨道衰变与圆化过程

origin.question:
[文件 1: 1_s18_18.7.md]

Problem 18.7. Two point masses $\mathfrak{m}_1$ and $\mathfrak{m}_2$ are in a Newtonian elliptical orbit with semimajor axis $a$ and eccentricity $e$ . Compute $da/dt$ and $de/dt$ due to gravitational radiation reaction. Show that the elliptical orbit tends to be circularized.

## Solution
origin.answer: \\left\\langle \\frac{\\mathrm{d} a}{\\mathrm{d} t}\\right\\rangle = -\\frac{64}{5} \\frac{m_1 m_2(m_1+m_2)}{a^3(1-e^2)^{7/2}} \\left(1+\\frac{73}{24}e^2+\\frac{37}{96}e^4\\right) \\\\ \\left\\langle \\frac{\\mathrm{d} e}{\\mathrm{d} t}\\right\\rangle = -\\frac{304}{15} \\frac{m_1 m_2(m_1+m_2)e}{a^4(1-e^2)^{5/2}} \\left(1+\\frac{121}{304}e^2\\right) \\\\ \\text{结论: } \\frac{de}{dt} < 0 \\text{，轨道偏心率随时间单调减小，椭圆轨道趋于圆化。}
origin.solution:
Solution 18.7. In terms of the total orbital energy $\mathbf{E}$ and angular momentum $\mathbf{L}$ , the semimajor axis and eccentricity are
$$
a = - m_{1} m_{1} / 2 E 	ag {1}
$$
$$
e^{2} = 1 + \frac {2 E L^{2} \left(m_{1} + m_{2}\right)}{m_{1}^{3} m_{2}^{3}} 	ag {2}
$$
so that
$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} a}{\mathrm {d} t} = \frac {m_{1} m_{2}}{2 E^{2}} \frac {\mathrm {d} E}{\mathrm {d} t} 	ag {3}
$$
$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {e}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \frac {\mathrm {m}_{1} + \mathrm {m}_{2}}{\mathrm {m}_{1}^{3} \mathrm {m}_{2}^{3} \mathrm {e}} \left(\mathrm {L}^{2} \frac {\mathrm {d E}}{\mathrm {d t}} + 2 \mathrm {E L} \frac {\mathrm {d L}}{\mathrm {d t}}\right). 	ag {4}
$$
If the separation of the two particles is $\mathbf{r}$ , where
$$
r = \frac {a \left(1 - e^{2}\right)}{1 + e \cos \theta} 	ag {5}
$$
(see figure), then
$$
\mathbf {r}_{1} = \frac {\mathbf {m}_{2}}{\mathbf {m}_{1} + \mathbf {m}_{2}} \mathbf {r}
$$
$$
\mathbf {r}_{2} = \frac {\mathbf {m}_{1}}{\mathbf {m}_{1} + \mathbf {m}_{2}} \mathbf {r}
$$
so the components of the quadrupole tensor are
$$
I_{x x} = m_{1} x_{1}^{2} + m_{2} x_{2}^{2} = \frac {m_{1} m_{2}}{m_{1} + m_{2}} r^{2} \cos^{2} \theta
$$
![](images/c10df870c1f14883a972caca37b0c38a825b71f25231dc036ce9a146443dfb8f.jpg)
$$
\mathbf {I}_{\mathbf {y y}} = \frac {\mathbf {m_{1}} \mathbf {m_{2}}}{\mathbf {m_{1}} + \mathbf {m_{2}}} \mathbf {r}^{2} \sin^{2} \theta
$$
$$
I_{x y} = \frac {m_{1} m_{2}}{m_{1} + m_{2}} r^{2} \sin \theta \cos \theta 	ag {6}
$$
$$
\mathbf {I} \equiv \mathbf {I_{x x}} + \mathbf {I_{y y}} = \frac {\mathbf {m_{1}} \mathbf {m_{2}}}{\mathbf {m_{1}} + \mathbf {m_{2}}} \mathbf {\Omega r^{2}} .
$$
According to the Newtonian equations of motion
$$
\dot {\theta} = \frac {\left[ \left(\mathrm {m}_{1} + \mathrm {m}_{2}\right) \mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right) \right]^{\frac {1}{2}}}{\mathrm {r}^{2}} 	ag {7}
$$
so Equation (5) gives
$$
\dot {\mathbf {r}} = \mathrm {e} \sin \theta \left[ \frac {\mathrm {m}_{1} + \mathrm {m}_{2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)} \right]^{\frac {1}{2}}. 	ag {8}
$$
Now we compute the successive time derivatives of $\mathbf{I}_{ij}$ , using Equations (5), (7) and (8) to simplify the expressions. The results are
$$
\dot {\mathrm {I}_{\mathbf {x x}}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2} \mathrm {r} \cos \theta \sin \theta}{[ (\mathrm {m}_{1} + \mathrm {m}_{2}) \mathrm {a} (1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}) ]^{\frac {1}{2}}} 	ag {9}
$$
$$
\ddot {\mathrm {I}_{\mathbf {x x}}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)} (\cos 2 \theta + \mathrm {e} \cos^{3} \theta) 	ag {10}
$$
$$
\dot {\mathrm {I}_{\mathbf {x x}}} = \frac {2 \mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)} (2 \sin 2 \theta + 3 \mathrm {e} \cos^{2} \theta \sin \theta) \dot {\theta} 	ag {11}
$$
$$
\dot {\mathbf {I}_{\mathbf {y y}}} = \frac {2 \mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2}}{\left[ \left(\mathrm {m}_{1} + \mathrm {m}_{2}\right) \mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right) \right]^{\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm {r} (\sin \theta \cos \theta + \mathrm {e} \sin \theta) 	ag {12}
$$
$$
\ddot {\mathrm {I}_{\mathrm {y y}}} = \frac {2 \mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)} \left(\cos 2 \theta + \mathrm {e} \cos \theta + \mathrm {e} \cos^{3} \theta + \mathrm {e}^{2}\right) 	ag {13}
$$
$$
\dot {\mathbf {I}_{\mathbf {y y}}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)} (2 \sin 2 \theta + \mathrm {e} \sin \theta + 3 \mathrm {e} \cos^{2} \theta \sin \theta) \dot {\theta} 	ag {14}
$$
$$
\dot {\mathbf {I}_{\mathbf {x y}}} = \frac {\mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2} \mathrm {r} \left(\cos^{2} \theta - \sin^{2} \theta + \mathrm {e} \cos \theta\right)}{\left[ \left(\mathrm {m}_{1} + \mathrm {m}_{2}\right) \mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right) \right]^{\frac {1}{2}}} 	ag {15}
$$
$$
\ddot {\mathrm {I}_{\mathrm {x y}}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)} (\sin 2 \theta + \mathrm {e} \sin \theta + \mathrm {e} \sin \theta \cos^{2} \theta) 	ag {16}
$$
$$
\dot {\mathrm {I}_{\mathrm {x y}}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)} (2 \cos 2 \theta - \mathrm {e} \cos \theta + 3 \mathrm {e} \cos^{3} \theta) \dot {\theta} 	ag {17}
$$
$$
\cdots \mathrm {I} = \cdots \mathrm {I}_{\mathbf {x x}} + \cdots \mathrm {I}_{\mathbf {y y}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2} \mathrm {e} \sin \theta \dot {\theta}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)}. 	ag {18}
$$
The rate of loss of energy is then:
$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} = - \frac {1}{5} \overset {\dot s} {\mathbf {I}_{\mathbf {j k}}}^{\dot s} \overset {\dot s} {\mathbf {I}_{\mathbf {j k}}} = - \frac {1}{5} \left(\overset {\dot s} {\mathbf {I}_{\mathbf {j k}}}^{\dot s} \overset {\dot s} {\mathbf {I}_{\mathbf {j k}}} - \frac {1}{3} \overset {\dot s} {\mathbf {I}}^{2}\right) \\ = - \frac {1}{5} \left(\stackrel {\dot s} {\text bf {I}_{\text bf {x x}}}^{2} + 2 \stackrel {\dot s} {\text bf {I}_{\text bf {x y}}}^{2} + \stackrel {\dot s} {\text bf {I}_{\text bf {y y}}}^{2} - \frac {1}{3} \stackrel {\dot s} {\text bf {I}}^{2}\right) \\ = \frac {- 8 m_{1}^{2} m_{2}^{2}}{1 5 a^{2} (1 - e^{2})^{2}} \left[ 1 2 (1 + e \cos \theta )^{2} + e^{2} \sin^{2} \theta \right] \dot {\theta}^{2} \\ \end{array}
$$
where we have used Equations (9)-(18) and straightforward algebraic simplifications. Now we must average over an orbit. The period of an orbit is (Kepler's third law)
$$
\mathrm {T} = \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac {1}{\dot {\theta}} \mathrm {d} \theta = \frac {2 \pi \mathrm {a}^{\frac {3}{2}}}{(\mathrm {m}_{1} + \mathrm {m}_{2})^{\frac {1}{2}}}
$$
so that
$$
\begin{array}{l} \left\langle \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} \right\rangle = \frac {1}{\mathrm {T}} \int_{0}^{\mathrm {T}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} \mathrm {d} t = \frac {1}{\mathrm {T}} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {1}{\dot {\theta}} \mathrm {d} \theta \\ = \frac {- 3 2}{5} \frac {\mathrm {m}_{1}^{2} \mathrm {m}_{2}^{2} \left(\mathrm {m}_{1} + \mathrm {m}_{2}\right)}{\mathrm {a}^{5} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)^{7 / 2}} \left(1 + \frac {7 3}{2 4} \mathrm {e}^{2} + \frac {3 7}{9 6} \mathrm {e}^{4}\right). 	ag {19}. \\ \end{array}
$$
Similarly, the rate of loss of angular momentum is
$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {d L}{d t} = \frac {- 2}{5} \varepsilon_{z i j} \ddot {\mathbf {I}_{i k}} \dddot {\mathbf {I}_{k j}} = \frac {- 2}{5} \varepsilon_{z i j} \dddot {\mathbf {I}_{i k}} \dddot {\mathbf {I}_{k j}} \\ = \frac {- 2}{5} \left[ \overset {\prime} {I}_{\mathbf {x y}} \left(\overset {\prime} {I}_{\mathbf {y y}} - \overset {\prime} {I}_{\mathbf {x x}}\right) + \overset {\prime} {I}_{\mathbf {x y}} \left(\overset {\prime} {I}_{\mathbf {x x}} - \overset {\prime} {I}_{\mathbf {y y}}\right) \right] \\ = \frac {- 8}{5} \frac {m_{1}^{2} m_{2}^{2}}{a^{2} \left(1 - e^{2}\right)^{2}} [ 4 + 1 0 e \cos \theta + e^{2} (9 \cos^{2} \theta - 1) \\ + \mathrm {e}^{3} (3 \cos^{3} \theta - \cos \theta) ] \dot {\theta} \\ \end{array}
$$
which when time averaged gives us
$$
\begin{array}{l} \left\langle \frac {\mathrm {d} L}{\mathrm {d} t} \right\rangle = \frac {1}{T} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac {\mathrm {d} L}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {1}{\dot {\theta}} \mathrm {d} \theta \\ = \frac {- 3 2}{5} \frac {\mathrm {m}_{1}^{2} \mathrm {m}_{2}^{2} \left(\mathrm {m}_{1} + \mathrm {m}_{2}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}}}{\mathrm {a}^{7 / 2} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)^{2}} \left(1 + \frac {7}{8} \mathrm {e}^{2}\right). 	ag {20}. \\ \end{array}
$$
From Equations (3) and (4) we have, finally:
$$
\begin{array}{l} \left\langle \frac {\mathrm {d} a}{\mathrm {d} t} \right\rangle = \frac {2 a^{2}}{\mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2}} \left\langle \frac {\mathrm {d} E}{\mathrm {d} t} \right\rangle \\ = \frac {- 6 4}{5} \frac {\mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2} \left(\mathrm {m}_{1} + \mathrm {m}_{2}\right)}{\mathrm {a}^{3} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)^{7 / 2}} \left(1 + \frac {7 3}{2 4} \mathrm {e}^{2} + \frac {3 7}{9 6} \mathrm {e}^{4}\right) 	ag {21} \\ \end{array}
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l} \left\langle \frac {d e}{d t} \right\rangle = \frac {m_{1} + m_{2}}{m_{1} m_{2} e} \left[ \frac {a (1 - e^{2})}{m_{1} + m_{2}} \left\langle \frac {d E}{d t} \right\rangle - \frac {\left(1 - e^{2}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}}}{a^{\frac {1}{2}} \left(m_{1} + m_{2}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}}} \left\langle \frac {d L}{d t} \right\rangle \right] \\ = \frac {- 3 0 4}{1 5} \frac {\mathrm {m}_{1} \mathrm {m}_{2} \left(\mathrm {m}_{1} + \mathrm {m}_{2}\right) \mathrm {e}}{\mathrm {a}^{4} \left(1 - \mathrm {e}^{2}\right)^{5 / 2}} \left(1 + \frac {1 2 1}{3 0 4} \mathrm {e}^{2}\right). 	ag {22}. \\ \end{array}
$$
From Equation (22) we see that $\mathrm{de} / \mathrm{dt}$ is negative, so the eccentricity decreases because of radiation reaction.
If we put $e = 0$ in Equation (21), we recover the results of Problem 18.6. For the integration of Equations (21) and (22) when $e \neq 0$ , see P. C. Peters, Phys. Rev. 136, 1224 (1964).
